title: Women are living longer-policies can help them live better
gist: Women dominate the aging population (54% of those 60+, 62% of those 80+) and the number of women 60+ in developing countries will more than double from 400 million to 900 million by 2050, but they age with more income insecurity, worse health, and greater unmet care needs, creating investment opportunities in silver-economy finance, NCD health systems, and paid care services.

insights.claim: Women comprise 54 percent of the global population aged 60 and above and 62 percent of the population above 80.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: The aging market is disproportionately female, so products and services for the elderly should be designed around women's needs and longevity.
insights.quote: Women comprise 54 percent of global population aged 60 and above, and 62 percent of the global population above 80.

insights.claim: There are 400 million women aged 60 or above in developing countries today, projected to reach 900 million by 2050.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: A more-than-doubling of older women in emerging markets by 2050 is a large secular demand pool for elder-care, health, pensions, and silver-economy finance.
insights.quote: There are 400 million women aged 60 or above in developing countries today, a figure projected to reach 900 million by 2050.

insights.claim: Women in low-income countries will live seven years longer than their mothers did, and those in middle-income countries three years more, but these added years are not necessarily healthy ones.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Rising female longevity without matching health gains creates a widening gap between lifespan and healthspan that policy and health-services markets must fill.
insights.quote: Women in low-income countries will live seven years more than their mothers did.
insights.locator: Women are living longer, but not necessarily better

insights.claim: In lower-middle-income countries only 41 percent of retired women receive a pension versus 55 percent of men, and women's pensions are often lower in value due to fewer working years and lower salaries.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: A large uninsured older-female cohort points to demand for alternative savings and insurance products and to fiscal pressure on public transfers.
insights.quote: 41 percent of retired women receive a pension in lower-middle income countries, compared to 55 percent of men

insights.claim: A WHO survey found women perceive the same level of health as men a decade older, and older women suffer high rates of arthritis, osteoporosis, dementia, and depression.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Female-skewed chronic-disease burden shapes where healthcare demand and drug/device markets for the elderly will concentrate.
insights.quote: women perceive the same level of health as men a decade older

insights.claim: One in four women over age 65 lives alone, more than twice the share of men, driving higher social isolation and unmet basic-care needs.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: High rates of older women living alone create demand for home-based care, assistive services, and social-support models.
insights.quote: One in four women over age 65 lives alone, more than twice the share of men.

insights.claim: Older women's old-age income insecurity and poor health are not sudden but compound from lifetime gender wage gaps, limited asset accumulation, pension ineligibility, and early-life health-spending bias favoring sons.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Interventions targeting only old age miss the root causes; the payoff is highest from early and continuous investment in girls and working-age women.
insights.locator: Aging policy should not start in old age

insights.claim: A 'life-course' approach that addresses gender inequalities before they compound with age is proposed as more effective than aging policies focused only on old age or gender policies focused only on working and reproductive years.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Signals World Bank programming direction, integrating gender and aging lenses, useful for anticipating human-capital lending priorities.
insights.locator: Aging policies that solely focus on old-age

insights.claim: The IFC's Silver Economy work in Latin America supports financial products for older women such as flexible savings accounts, tailored insurance, and financial advisory, framed as household-resilience and job-creating investments.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Names an active development-finance thesis around older-women financial products, a potential co-investment or market-signal for silver-economy fintech.
insights.locator: In Latin America, the International Finance Corporation

insights.claim: In North Macedonia the World Bank-supported expansion of home-based care let more than 1,600 older people live more independently while creating paid care jobs, many taken by younger women from disadvantaged and minority communities.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the double dividend of care systems, elder independence plus female employment, a template for scalable paid-care markets.

insights.claim: The Kerala Health Systems Improvement Program in India targets improved life expectancy and quality of life for 11 million older people through wider health coverage and resilient systems.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Illustrates the scale of publicly-funded elder-health programs in emerging markets, a demand signal for health-system suppliers and screening/diagnostics.
insights.locator: The Kerala Health Systems Improvement Program

insights.claim: The authors argue ensuring older women's welfare is an investment rather than an expense, and that the most effective strategies invest early and continuously in girls' education, women's health, fair labor markets, and asset accumulation.
insights.speaker: Wendy Cunningham
insights.so_what: Positions older-women welfare spending as productivity-enhancing, shaping how development budgets justify human-capital allocation.
insights.quote: The most effective strategies invest early and continuously
insights.locator: Ensuring older women's welfare is an investment
